paragraphs.text: 1.1 We welcome the opportunity to respond to the European Commission's (EC) call for evidence in relation to a proposed Digital Fairness Act (the DFA).

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
1. Summary

paragraphs.text: 1.2 We support the EC's objective to ensure that consumer protection rules remain fit for purpose in the digital age. We also agree that the current regulatory landscape is challenging, with "unclear rules for businesses and market fragmentation leading to higher business costs, obstacles to cross-border trade/missed business opportunities and unfair competition". However, the EC should only pursue new legislation if that new legislation can rationalise and simplify the regulatory environment. Neither consumers nor businesses will benefit from merely adding a new layer of regulation.

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
1. Summary

evidence_quotes.quote: the EC's proposal will be principles-based

paragraphs.text: 1.3 We are somewhat encouraged by the confirmation by EC director Isabelle Pérignon that the EC's proposal will be principles-based, as we consider that a prescriptive set of rules would risk increasing rather than mitigating the significant legal uncertainty in this area, and would likely result in regulatory duplication, overreach and fragmentation. Any new legislative proposal should therefore seek to:

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
1. Summary

evidence_quotes.quote: principles-based regulation instead of prescriptive rules

paragraphs.text: (i) adopt principles-based regulation instead of prescriptive rules;

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
1. Summary

paragraphs.text: (ii) avoid unnecessary intervention; and

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
1. Summary

paragraphs.text: (iii) ensure the regulation is backed by proportionate and predictable enforcement.

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
1. Summary

paragraphs.text: Focusing on these three points will help strike the right balance between robust protection for consumers, and legal certainty for businesses operating in the digital economy.

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
1. Summary

paragraphs.text: 2.1 As the call for evidence itself notes, "[a]s a result of the development of EU consumer law over the last 50 years, EU consumers are among the most protected in the world, online and offline". In drawing up a legislative proposal for the DFA, the EC must take due account of the plethora of existing interrelated rules and regulations. EU law already regulates businesses' interactions with consumers in the digital sphere via regulations including the Digital Services Act, Digital Markets Act, and AI Act, whilst key pre-digital consumer protection rules are still also in effect. The EC must therefore proceed with caution as it conducts its impact assessment for the DFA in order to avoid creating new

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
2. Principles-based regulation will aid legal certainty

paragraphs.text: legislation that exacerbates legal uncertainty and further complicates the compliance efforts of businesses already striving to comply with complex existing rules.

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
2. Principles-based regulation will aid legal certainty

paragraphs.text: 2.2 Any proposal should therefore prioritise the consolidation and simplification of the current multi-layered digital regulation landscape. In drawing this up, the EC should focus on the key overarching principles that businesses should adhere to when interacting with consumers in the digital sphere, and avoid potentially duplicative prescriptive rules which risk simplifying nuanced consumer interactions and impeding legitimate business practices. Any proposal should focus on concepts and definitions which are clear and objective, with proportionality and flexibility built in where appropriate to avoid stifling innovation and competition. Such an approach has generally worked well in the more mature competition law space and would ensure that any new rules do not quickly become outdated in this fast-moving area. Given that the majority of businesses that would be regulated by the DFA operate across multiple Member States, a policy of simplification and coherence is particularly important.

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
2. Principles-based regulation will aid legal certainty

evidence_quotes.quote: Any proposal should avoid excessive intervention in fast-moving areas where savvy consumers are making well-informed choices.

paragraphs.text: 3.1 Any proposal should avoid excessive intervention in fast-moving areas where savvy consumers are making well-informed choices. In particular:

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
3. Excessive intervention risks harming competition and innovation

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns: we support the prohibition of materially misleading design practices

paragraphs.text: (i) Dark patterns: we support the prohibition of materially misleading design practices, but consider "dark patterns" as a concept to be difficult to define and emotionally charged, with significant scope for legal uncertainty. In regulating these practices the EC should focus on legislating against obvious consumer harm, rather than second-guessing consumer choices. We also note that many such practices are already covered by the existing EU consumer protection framework. For example, the CPC's sweep on dark patterns in 2022 found that at least 37% of the websites it checked potentially violated the UCPD due to the use of fake countdown timers, false hierarchies and/or hidden information.

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
3. Excessive intervention risks harming competition and innovation

evidence_quotes.quote: Fair design obligations: a principles-based approach should again be favoured over detailed compliance documentation, which would lead to regulatory drag and disproportionate costs for businesses, and risks undermining the drive for European competitiveness.

paragraphs.text: (ii) Fair design obligations: a principles-based approach should again be favoured over detailed compliance documentation, which would lead to regulatory drag and disproportionate costs for businesses, and risks undermining the drive for European competitiveness.

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
3. Excessive intervention risks harming competition and innovation

paragraphs.text: (iii) Personalisation and targeting: given the potential for personalisation to enhance competition and consumer outcomes, a more nuanced approach should be taken (rather than the blunt "personalisation = bad"), including by distinguishing between legitimate product differentiation and targeting consumer vulnerabilities.

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
3. Excessive intervention risks harming competition and innovation

paragraphs.text: 4.1 The call for evidence notes that "work needs to continue to address remaining shortcomings [...] ensuring effective enforcement". We agree that proportionate and

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
4. Proportionate and predictable enforcement is key

paragraphs.text: predictable enforcement is key to the success of the EU's digital consumer protection regime, with centralised enforcement (akin to that in the competition regime) significantly more effective than enforcement at a Member State level. Absent a 'one stop shop' for consumer protection enforcement, the EC should at a minimum push for increased cooperation between regulators. A simplified regime would by its nature aid the push for consistency in the interpretation of consumer protection rules by national authorities.

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
4. Proportionate and predictable enforcement is key

paragraphs.text: 4.2 Although the call for evidence suggests that considerations related to enforcement will feed into proposals, the impact assessment questionnaire itself contains strikingly few questions in relation to enforcement, suggesting that the EC may intend to tackle these issues as part of its separate review of the EU Consumer Protection Cooperation Regulation. If this is the intended approach, we urge the EC to keep front of mind the importance of consolidating and simplifying the regime as a whole whilst working on these separate reforms.

section_path: Slaughter and May response to European Commission Call for Evidence on a Proposed EU Digital Fairness Act
4. Proportionate and predictable enforcement is key
